observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer, MPD Coordinator and other concerned personnel ensure that the PPAs to be funded out of the 20% DF are procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation shall be considered.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, MPD Coordinator and Municipal Engineer fast-track and monitor the implementation of the PPAs funded out of the 20% Development Fund both for current and prior years’ balances to achieve the purpose of the fund.

We further recommended that appropriations of all PPAs which are no longer feasible and have not yet been implemented as well as those savings from already implemented PPAs, be reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the 20% DF for re-appropriation to other development projects pursuant to budgetary rules.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer together with the Appraisal and Disposal Committee, conduct immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, to prevent further deterioration and loss of additional income.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant derecognize/ drop from the books of accounts all disposed unserviceable properties after disposal and recognize gain/ loss on disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

GSO to: 

Prepare the SSMI and submit it together with the RIS to the accounting office as a basis for recording the expense portion of the inventories;

Municipal Accountant to:

record all regular purchases of supplies and materials as inventory using the perpetual inventory method

We recommended that the:

Municipal Treasurer to close the TF bank account specifically opened for donations and ensure the received donations in cash should be deposited in the bank account exclusively maintained for the DRRM Fund; 

Municipal Accountant to:

prepare and maintain separate subsidiary ledger (SL) for "Trust Liabilities-DRRMF" for cash donations received amounting to Pl00,000.00 and above per donor per purpose while those below Pl00,000.00 shall be grouped and posted in one SL labeled as "Various Donors" per purpose.

draw a Journal Entry Voucher to record the donations in cash in the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF account (2-04-01-020); and 

The General Service Officer to prepare and maintain:
a.	Issuance Form for In-Kind Donations-DRRM
b.	Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM
c.	Inventory List of all procured and donated items

We recommended that management should exert extra effort to prove the existence of the above cited accounts and if effort proved futile, a resolution from the Sangguniang Bayan should be passed to apply for the write-off of the accounts which should only be done upon prior authority from COA.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassified inventory items in the books of accounts; and
Property Officer to update the PCs such as changing the name of the responsible employee therein and disclosing that the items are already unserviceable and returned by the employee.
We recommended that the Municipal Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant continue undertaking collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Custodian and the PELCs maintained by the Accounting Office are reconciled.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer together with the Appraisal and Disposal Committee, conduct immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, to prevent further deterioration and loss of additional income.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant derecognize/ drop from the books of accounts all disposed unserviceable properties after disposal and recognize gain/ loss on disposal.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Engineer to regularly coordinate and submit the Final Inspection Report, Statement of Work Accomplished, As- Built Plans, Certificate of Turnover and pictures of the completed projects together with the Certificate of Completion to the Municipal Accounting Office, to ensure that completed projects are recorded in the books of accounts; and
The Municipal Accountant to trace the unidentified balances in the “CIP” accounts amounting to P2,654,652.49.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the supporting documents to prove the validity and propriety of recorded “Accounts Payable” in CY 2024 totaling P6,865,681.12. Henceforth, record only in the “Accounts Payable” obligations that are founded on valid claims properly supported by sufficient evidence.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the unsettled balance of P219,761.02 in the “Due to PhilHealth” account for prior years. Any confirmed outstanding balance must be remitted to PhilHealth immediately; and
Reclassify to the “Trust Liabilities” account the funds received from the national government intended for the expenses of the Municipal Rural Health Unit and professional fees amounting to P973,467.66 which was misclassified as “Due to PhilHealth” in the Trust Fund books.
We also recommended that the HRM Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
Ensure the full and timely remittance of the amounts withheld from officials and personnel as well as the share provided by the Municipality in accordance with the deadline set by PhilHealth;
Immediately remit to PhilHealth the total withheld contributions of municipal employees, amounting to P12,604.59 which remain unremitted as of this writing; and
Exert effort to locate the supporting documents of the unremitted premiums indicated in the SPA to immediately resolve the deficiencies noted.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant as the person responsible on the withholding and remittance of the PhilHealth premiums, refund the irregularly paid penalties incurred due to the late remittance of May and April 2024 contributions amounting to P13,577.87.
This is without prejudice to the issuance of the Notice of Disallowance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the accounting records to verify the unidentified difference between the balances of the Subsidiary Ledgers and General Ledgers of the “Due to GSIS” account and draw correcting entries if necessary.
We also recommended that the Payroll-In-Charge prepare the monthly payroll to ensure that salaries of Municipal officials and personnel are deducted with the correct amounts due for remittance to the GSIS.  The Payroll Officer   should also ensure that these deductions tally with the GSIS billing.
We recommended that the HRM Officer:
Promptly coordinate with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to update the municipal personnel’s employment status and/ or salary adjustments. This will ensure that GSIS records reflect accurate and up-to-date information; and
Inform the concerned municipal employees on the remitted premiums and loan payments without corresponding deductions from their salaries totaling P8,222.10. Require refund of payment to the Municipality and subsequently submit to the Audit Team proof of refund made.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the accounting records to verify the accuracy of the prior years’ unremitted balance of P125,099.72 under the “Due to Pag-IBIG” account. Any confirmed outstanding obligations should be remitted to Pag-IBIG immediately, and necessary adjustments should be made in the books, if applicable;
Ensure the correct recording in the books of the Pag-IBIG contributions withheld from employees, including the government share; and
Ensure the full and timely remittance of the amounts withheld from officials and personnel as well as the share provided by the Municipality in accordance with the deadline set by Pag-IBIG.
We also recommended that the Payroll-in-Charge prepare the monthly payroll and ensure that salaries of municipal officials and personnel are deducted with the correct amounts due for remittance to the Pag-IBIG. She should also ensure that these deductions tally with the monthly Pag-IBIG billing.
Lastly, we recommended that the Human Resource Management Officer:
Inform the concerned municipal officials/ employees on the remitted premiums without corresponding deductions in their salary and require refund of payment to the Municipality. Subsequently, submit proof of refund made to the Audit Team; and
Conduct reconciliation with the records of Pag-IBIG to determine of unfulfilled obligations both of the employee and the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning Development Coordinator: (MPDC)
Ensure that the PPAs under the   20% DF are well-planned, as well as procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation will be considered; and
Revert back to the unappropriated surplus of the 20% DF for re-appropriation to other development projects the unexpended appropriations for the procurement of corn and rice seeds which are no longer feasible for implementation totaling P200,000.00, as well as those savings from already implemented PPAs in CYs 2022 and 2023 amounting to P137,851.49 and P162,480.13, respectively, pursuant to budgetary rules and regulations.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the members of the LDRRM Council continuously adhere with the provisions of the aforementioned Circular to ensure maximum attainment of the purpose of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the members of the GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) and GAD Focal Person to:
Prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01;
Monitor actual expenditures to ensure the Municipality has sufficient funds for implementing all GAD activities and coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant in preparing the annual GAD Utilization Report; and
Refrain from charging non-GAD-related activities to the GAD budget thru coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that expenses not clearly addressing a specific gender issue are not charged to the GAD Budget.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor, together with the members of the Local Finance Committee:
Conduct a root-cause analysis to determine the underlying reasons for expenses exceeding revenues and based on the results, formulate concrete measures to prevent recurrence; and
Create and implement a plan to address the current deficit of P4,435,863.17.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Immediately compute the correct tax liability for each affected employee, encompassing the previously excluded BAC Honoraria. The corresponding under withheld amount should be reflected in a payroll adjustment and remitted to the BIR as soon as possible, along with any applicable penalties or interest; and
Carefully examine DVs and ensure that taxes from taxable compensations of Municipal employees are duly withhold to avoid the same lapses in the future.

We recommended that the Local School Board, headed by the Municipal Mayor:
Furnish the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer a copy of the SEF Budget within seven (7) days after its approval as their basis for disbursement or certification of availability of funds and for recording purposes; and
Earnestly submit the quarterly and annual utilization reports of the SEF Budget to the DepEd Central Office through its appropriate channel, copy furnished the local Sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and DILG within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that travel claims of JO workers are supported with a certification from the Head of the Agency or any authorized approving official stating that official missions/ tasks could not be performed by/ or assigned to any other regular/ permanent official and/ or employee; and that the tasks/ activities are necessary to fulfill the obligations as contained in their contract of service.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Supply Officer to:
Prepare annually the consolidated Property Inventory Form listing all the insurable properties and other assets of the Municipality; and
Submit the consolidated PIF to the Audit Team Leader and to the GIF, GSIS, not later than April 30 of each year as required in COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018 as basis for the assessment of general insurance coverage over all insurable assets, properties and interests of the Municipality with the GIF of the GSIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Inventory Committee and Municipal Accountant implement and follow the prescribed guidelines and procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 2020-006, particularly the conduct of reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records, for the purpose of one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant compute and recognize the depreciation expenses for the infrastructure assets.

Also, we recommended that the Supply Officer maintain and update the PCs on a regular basis.

Lastly, we recommended that the Supply Officer and Municipal Accountant:

Undertake collaborative procedures to ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that the PCs maintained by the Property Custodian and the PPELCs maintained by the Accounting Office are reconciled, and

Prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the Municipal Mayor for his appropriate action on non- existing/ missing properties.
observations.managementAction:
Reconciliation of balances was undertaken by the Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer although there were still remaining PPE account balances that remained unreconciled as of December 31, 2024.

Audit observations on the PPE account are discussed in Part II, Audit Observation No. 1.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure the correctness of amounts of collections and deposits to avoid under/ over deposit in the future.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to recognize the refunded unexpended Petty Cash Fund balance by the Petty Cash Custodian,

Prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to record the reconciling items noted in the Bank Reconciliation Statements to correct Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Accounts balances, and

Prepare and maintain Subsidiary Ledgers for each of the bank accounts under the Cash in Bank- Local Currency, Current and Savings accounts held by the Municipality

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Supply Officer to:

exert effort in locating the supporting documents of the unsubstantiated lot recorded in the books, and

furnish the Municipal Accountant copies of Original/ Transfer Certificates of Title, Deeds of Absolute Sale, Tax Declarations and other pertinent documents supporting ownership of parcels of land by the Municipality as the latter’s basis in recognizing the unrecorded lots in the Land account.

Municipal Accountant:

to trace and reconcile the amounts recorded in the books with the source documents and effect adjustments, if necessary.

Municipal Assessor:

to expedite the processing of Certificate of Title of the untitled lots in order to establish absolute ownership and protect the legal interest of the Municipality.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure and establish lot ownership, in the name of the Municipality, before construction of any structures and land improvements.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council thru the LDRRM Officer discontinue the inclusion of PPAs in the LDRRM Investment Plan that are not related to disaster preparedness, mitigation and response.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

together with the LDRRM Officer, ensure optimal utilization of LDRRM Fund thru implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan and 

stop the approval of payment for expenses which are not related to LDRRM activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

thru the OIC- Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, hasten the implementation of the Supplemental Feeding Program funded by the DWSD FO VIII for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries, and

require the Municipal Accountant to identify the Source Agencies of the "Balances from Various Projects" for their return. Otherwise, initiate the request for their write-off pursuant to COA Circular 2016-006 dated December 19, 2016, once it is identified that these balances have been on the books for 10 years or more.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the disbursement voucher for the return of the unexpended amount of P311,851.00 to the DOLE.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary journal entries to transfer the 20% share from Fire Code Fees to the Trust Liabilities account.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:

as the designated Special Disbursing Officer, require representatives of claimants of funds in the same nature to submit authorization and identification documents prescribed in DSWD MC Nos. 04 series of 2019, 16 series of 2021 and COA Circular 2012-001;

submit to the Audit Team copies of the authorization and identification documents of the representatives of the claimants for the released social pension to support the Fund Utilization Reports; and

We also recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer conduct reassessment and close monitoring of the qualification/ status of each beneficiary to avoid undue payment of claims under the Social Pension Program for indigent senior citizens.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) and all other municipal health workers to:

secure Certification from the Secretary of the DOH that the place of assignment/travel is a strife-torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption, and

submit the following documents:

Special Order from the agency/ department head covering the assignment to hazardous/ difficult areas,
lacking DTRs, and
authority to collect for initial benefit claims of Subsistence and Laundry Allowances approved by the Agency Head

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the guidelines on the grant of Hazard Pay, Subsistence and Laundry Allowances.

Likewise, it is recommended that the Public Health Workers refund the overpayment of benefits they received.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to deduct retention money in the amount equivalent to at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of every progress payment or require the supplier/ contractor to post a special bank guarantee equivalent to at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of the total contract price of the goods procured, in order to protect the interest of the government and third parties involved in the execution of government projects, programs or activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:

Stop the payment of confidential expenses thru reimbursement. The release and utilization of the funds shall be thru a cash advance by a Special Disbursing Officer or the Head of the Agency, and

Comply with the aforementioned reporting requirements, upon request and grant of cash advance and its liquidation.

We recommended that BAC furnish the Audit Team with copies of approved contracts and all documents forming part thereof within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman and Municipal Accountant ensure that the basic information on the Purchase Orders are completely and appropriately filled- up, including the date of conformity of the supplier.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and MDRRM Officer provide for means to properly and safely store food items for long term storage that are intended for distribution in times of calamity.

observations.reason: An audit observation on the PPE account is discussed in Part II Observation No. 1 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to:

prepare the annual Schedule of the Delinquent Stallholders and Lessees as at year-end and furnish the same to the Municipal Accountant; and

prepare Demand Letters indicating the total unpaid monthly rentals and the penalties thereof to all delinquent stallholders.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of accounts the receivables on the rentals from the Market Stall and the Government Lots, based on the report forwarded by the Municipal Treasurer and the corresponding income earned therefrom.
observations.reason: No list of unpaid rentals and penalties from the municipal stalls for CY 2023 was submitted by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to the Municipal Accountant’s Office for the recording of receivables.

During the Audit Exit Conference, the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and Supply Officer agreed to exert effort to comply with the recommendations given by the Audit Team.

b.  	Municipal Accountant to trace and reconcile the amounts recorded in the books with the source documents and effect adjustments, if necessary.
c. 	Municipal Assessor to expedite the processing of Certificate of Title of the untitled lots in order to establish absolute ownership and protect the legal interest of the Municipality.

The Municipal Mayor, Supply Officer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Assessor noted the foregoing audit observations and committed to comply with the recommendations given.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Engineer, MPD Coordinator and other concerned personnel ensure that the PPAs to be funded out of the 20% DF are procurement-and-implementation-ready such that the factors that may impede implementation shall be considered.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, MPD Coordinator and Municipal Engineer fast-track and monitor the implementation of the PPAs funded out of the 20% Development Fund both for current and prior years’ balances to achieve the purpose of the fund.

With regard to the unimplemented PPAs funded by the continuing appropriation of the 20% DF, the Municipal Mayor stated that they will assess the feasibility of the projects for implementation.    If they are found to be no longer feasible, they will consider in realigning the funds to other identified development projects.

We recommended that the LDRRM Council thru the LDRRM Officer discontinue the inclusion of PPAs in the LDRRM Investment Plan that are not related to disaster preparedness, mitigation and response.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
together with the LDRRM Officer, ensure optimal utilization of LDRRM Fund thru implementation of all approved PPAs incorporated in the LDRRMF Investment Plan and
stop the approval of payment for expenses which are not related to LDRRM activities.
The Municipal Mayor and LDRRM Officer committed to ensure optimal utilization of the LDRRM Fund for the ensuing years. Also, the LDRRM Officer agreed to discontinue the inclusion of PPAs in the LDRRM Investment Plan that are not related to disaster preparedness, mitigation and response activities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
thru the OIC- Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer, hasten the implementation of the Supplemental Feeding Program funded by the DWSD FO VIII for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries, and
require the Municipal Accountant to identify the Source Agencies of the "Balances from Various Projects" for their return. Otherwise, initiate the request for their write-off pursuant to COA Circular 2016-006 dated December 19, 2016, once it is identified that these balances have been on the books for 10 years or more.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare the disbursement voucher for the return of the unexpended amount of ₱311,851.00 to the DOLE.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary journal entries to transfer the 20% share from Fire Code Fees to the Trust Liabilities account.
The representative of the MSWD Officer expressed her commitment to implement the Supplemental Feeding Program as soon as possible.
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer were amenable to the recommendations stated in paragraph nos. 6.21 and 6.22.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
as the designated Special Disbursing Officer, require representatives of claimants of funds in the same nature to submit authorization and identification documents prescribed in DSWD MC Nos. 04 series of 2019, 16 series of 2021 and COA Circular 2012-001;
submit to the Audit Team copies of the authorization and identification documents of the representatives of the claimants for the released social pension to support the Fund Utilization Reports; and
We also recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer conduct reassessment and close monitoring of the qualification/ status of each beneficiary to avoid undue payment of claims under the Social Pension Program for indigent senior citizens.
The Municipal Treasurer agreed to submit to the Audit Team copies of the authorization and identification documents of the representatives of the claimants for the released social pension to support the submitted Fund Utilization Reports.
Also, the representative of the MSWD Officer said that their office will coordinate with the OSCA in the reassessment and monitoring of the Social Pension beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer (MHO) and all other municipal health workers to:
secure Certification from the Secretary of the DOH that the place of assignment/travel is a strife-torn/embattled/disease-infested/distress or isolated areas/stations, or areas declared under state of calamity or emergency, or with volcano activity or eruption, and
submit the following documents:
Special Order from the agency/ department head covering the assignment to hazardous/ difficult areas,
lacking DTRs, and
authority to collect for initial benefit claims of Subsistence and Laundry Allowances approved by the Agency Head
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the guidelines on the grant of Hazard Pay, Subsistence and Laundry Allowances.
Likewise, it is recommended that the Public Health Workers refund the overpayment of benefits they received.
The Municipal Health Officer admitted their lapses and agreed to the foregoing audit observations. She further said that her office will comply with the recommendations given.
The Municipal Accountant also said that her office will coordinate with the Rural Health Unit so that claims for hazard pay, laundry and subsistence allowances will be in accordance with rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to deduct retention money in the amount equivalent to at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of every progress payment or require the supplier/ contractor to post a special bank guarantee equivalent to at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of the total contract price of the goods procured, in order to protect the interest of the government and third parties involved in the execution of government projects, programs or activities.
During the exit conference the BAC Chairman referred the Audit Team to Section 54.5 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 which provides that for Small Value Procurement, the Procuring Entity may require Warranty Security depending on the nature of the procurement project.
With this regard, the Audit Team emphasized that no threshold was provided in the Revised IRR on when to require Warranty Security. For Small Value Procurement, referring to the cited section, the Municipality can still require Warranty Security if upon their judgement, depending on the nature of the procurement project, such would be advantageous to the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
Stop the payment of confidential expenses thru reimbursement. The release and utilization of the funds shall be thru a cash advance by a Special Disbursing Officer or the Head of the Agency, and
Comply with the aforementioned reporting requirements, upon request and grant of cash advance and its liquidation.
The Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer understood the intentions of the foregoing audit observations. They committed to the adhere with the recommendations given by the Audit Team.

We recommended that BAC furnish the Audit Team with copies of approved contracts and all documents forming part thereof within five days from their execution as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009.
We recommended that the BAC Chairman and Municipal Accountant ensure that the basic information on the Purchase Orders are completely and appropriately filled- up, including the date of conformity of the supplier.
The BAC Chairman concurred with the recommendations provided and said that the Committee will strive to submit copies of contracts and Purchase Orders within the prescribed period. She and the Municipal Accountant also agreed to ensure that Purchase Orders are completely and appropriately filled- up for the enforcement of the Purchase Order’s terms and conditions for defaulting suppliers/ contractors.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer together with the Appraisal and Disposal Committee, conduct immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, to prevent further deterioration and loss of additional income.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant derecognize/ drop from the books of accounts all disposed unserviceable properties after disposal and recognize gain/ loss on disposal.
The Supply Officer agreed on the conduct of the immediate disposal of all unserviceable properties once members of the Appraisal and Disposal Committee attended the Seminar on Appraisal and Disposal of Government Property.
The Municipal Accountant also noted the recommendation stated in paragraph no. 12.8.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and MDRRM Officer provide for means to properly and safely store food items for long term storage that are intended for distribution in times of calamity.
The LDRRM Officer said during the Audit Exit Conference that actions were immediately taken by his office after his receipt of AOM No. 2024- 07 that discussed the foregoing observation. He added that the noted packs of rice were already distributed after checking and testing that they were still safe for consumption to avoid further spoilage and wastage.
The Municipal Mayor and the LDRRM Officer agreed to provide the means to properly and safely store food items for long term storage that are intended for distribution in times of calamity

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to send a written confirmation to the bank for the details of the outstanding loan balance, and make an adjusting entry to correct the Loans Payable balances in the books.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize Accounts Payable that are consummated transactions supported with complete documentation to prove that these are valid liabilities.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Direct the Municipal Accountant to:

record every January of each year the full amount of RPT due and collectible as receivables based on the computed amount per RPT Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s index cards; and 

reconcile the RPT/SET Receivables accounts with the Municipal Treasurer’s list of RPT delinquencies. Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries, if any, in order to present the correct balance of the account. 

Require the Municipal Assessor to:
prepare and submit to the Municipal Treasurer, on or before the thirty-first (31st) day of December each year, an assessment roll containing a list of all persons whose real properties have been newly assessed or reassessed and the values of such properties pursuant to RA No. 7160;  

verify and determine whether all real properties within the province are properly listed in the assessment roll; and 

update and reconcile all records pertaining to real properties within the Municipality and its owners. 

Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:

furnish the Municipal Accountant of a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible at the beginning of the year; 

submit the Certified list of Real Property Tax Delinquencies to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) on or before December 31 of the year immediately succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, and request for assistance from the SB in the enforcement of the remedies for collection provided in RA No. 7160; and

actively implement tax collection methods and strategies to increase RPT collections

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant check thoroughly the completeness of the supporting documents on the progress payments and the release of retention money.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer require the substitution of an appropriate securities enumerated in the IRR of RA 9184 on the release of retention money to the contractors.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer reconcile the Quarterly Report on Publicized Projects with the supporting documents attached in the final progress payment such as the Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA), Inspection Report and the Certificate of Completion to avoid contradicting data.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor, through the BAC to submit written justification why award of contract was made despite having poor performance in its on-going contracts as disclosed in the Statement of All On-going Government and Private Contracts including Contracts Awarded But Not Yet Started.

We also recommended to the BAC to submit written justification why a recommendation for an award of contract was made despite the misrepresentation of data in the bidding documents and the omission of data in the Statement of On-going Contracts Including Contracts Awarded but not yet started. 

Lastly, in future procurements of infrastructure projects, we recommended to the BAC to thoroughly scrutinize the post-qualification of the bidding documents and to require, where necessary, documents to comprehensively undertake the post-qualification of bidders.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal HRMO and the Municipal Budget Officer provide funds to adjust the current pay rate of the JO workers in accordance with the mandated daily minimum wage prescribed by the RTWPB for Region VIII.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Public Health Nurse and the Municipal Health Officer as Vaccine Coordinators to:

Submit to the Audit Team the report of the total vaccines received, and the lacking documentation and reports relative to the receipt and distribution of the covid vaccines and ancillary supplies such as: inspection and acceptance reports, distribution reports, master list of vaccinated individuals, and Inventory of Vaccines as of December 31, 2022; and 

Henceforth, ensure an up-to-date recording and record keeping as procedural controls for transparency and accountability in the use of government resources.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare without fail the required subsidiary ledgers for the account Due to NGAs as required under Section 10 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs to substantiate the details of the GL balance;

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the:

conduct the complete physical count of all properties, prepare the

recorded by the Municipal Accountant to ensure the existence,
completeness and accuracy reported PPE accounts in the financial
statement;

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to send a written confirmation to the bank for the details of the outstanding
loan balance, and make an adjusting entry to correct the Loans Payable
balances in the books.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
recognize Accounts Payable that are consummated transactions supported
with complete documentation to prove that these are valid liabilities.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor:
1. Direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) record every January of each year the full amount of RPT due and
collectible as receivables based on the computed amount per RPT Tax
Account Register/Taxpayer’s index cards; and
b) reconcile the RPT/SET Receivables accounts with the Municipal Treasurer’s
list of RPT delinquencies. Prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries, if
any, in order to present the correct balance of the account.
2. Require the Municipal Assessor to:
a) prepare and submit to the Municipal Treasurer, on or before the thirty-first
(31st) day of December each year, an assessment roll containing a list of all
persons whose real properties have been newly assessed or reassessed and the
values of such properties pursuant to RA No. 7160;
b) verify and determine whether all real properties within the province are
properly listed in the assessment roll; and
c) update and reconcile all records pertaining to real properties within the
Municipality and its owners.
3. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:
a) furnish the Municipal Accountant of a duly certified list showing the name of
taxpayers and the amount due and collectible at the beginning of the year;
b) submit the Certified list of Real Property Tax Delinquencies to the
Sangguniang Bayan (SB) on or before December 31 of the year immediately
succeeding the year in which the delinquencies were incurred, and request for
assistance from the SB in the enforcement of the remedies for collection
provided in RA No. 7160; and
c) actively implement tax collection methods and strategies to increase RPT
collections

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to:
a) prepare the annual Schedule of the Delinquent Stallholders and Lessees as
at year-end and furnish the same to the Municipal Accountant; and
b) prepare Demand Letters indicating the total unpaid monthly rentals and
the penalties thereof to all delinquent stallholders.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record in the books of
accounts the receivables on the rentals from the Market Stall and the
Government Lots, based on the report forwarded by the Municipal Treasurer and
the corresponding income earned therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant check thoroughly the
completeness of the supporting documents on the progress payments and the
release of retention money.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer require the substitution
of an appropriate securities enumerated in the IRR of RA 9184 on the release
of retention money to the contractors.

Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer reconcile the Quarterly
Report on Publicized Projects with the supporting documents attached in the
final progress payment such as the Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA),
Inspection Report and the Certificate of Completion to avoid contradicting
data.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant:
a. to exert all effort to locate the documents to support this account;
b. to send demand letters to those employees who have not yet settled their
disallowances, and,
c. to strictly monitor the settlement of suspension and disallowances.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to:
a. prepare journal entry voucher to record the provision of allowance for
impairment loss for the non-collection and non-liquidation, to fairly present
the Other Receivables-Due From NGOs/POs account in the Financial
Statements and
b. make a request for write-off to the Commission on Audit with all the pertinent
documents.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to send within ten (10) days before the
expiration of the prescribed period a written reminder under signature of the
LCE or his duly authorized representative enjoining officials and employees
concerned to settle their cash advance.
b. cause the immediate withholding of salaries and claims of officers and
employees who failed to settle their cash advances within the prescribed
period pursuant to Section 5.1.3 and 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002 after
being sent notice and demand. The Municipal Accountant is authorized to
delete the name of the official and/or employee from the subsequent payroll
until such time that the cash advance has been fully liquidated pursuant to
COA Circular No. 96-004.
c. direct the Municipal Accountat, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget
Officer to refrain from signing cash advance vouchers and obligation
requests of officials and employees with outstanding cash advances.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to maintain a special account in the General Fund for all loans availed of by
the municipality.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a. require the Municipal Accountant to maintain a special account in the
general fund and a separate depository checking account be opened for the
20% DF;
b. require the Municipal Treasurer to maintain a separate cash book, bank
book and accountable forms, such as but not limited to official receipts and
checks, to acknowledge receipt of the transfer of fund from the GF, and for
the disbursements thereon, respectively; and
c. require the Municipal Accountant to:
a) open and maintain a separate bookkeeping record for the 20% DF as a
special account of the GF and record thereon, all transactions pertaining
to the 20% DF;
b) maintain a separate series of Reports/Journals of Collections, Cash &
Check Disbursements, Vouchers, Payrolls, etc. for the 20% DF Special
Account;
c) record the transactions affecting the fund in accordance with PPSAS
Chart of accounts such as, recording the transfer as Subsidy to General
Fund Proper/Special Accounts - 20% DF (5.02.14.070, and, the receipt
of the subsidy in the Special Account as Subsidy from General Fund
Proper/Other Special Accounts (4.03.01.050), and so on;
d) prepare a separate periodic trial balance, financial reports, financial
statements, and bank reconciliation statements of the fund; and
e) incorporate in the GF its quarterly and year-end financial statements.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require:
a. the Municipal DRRM Officer - Designate to exclude the aforementioned
expenditures on the Annual LDRRMO Plan and Budget in accordance with
Item 4.3 to strictly comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 10121; and
b. Return the amount of ₱177,232.67 to the LDRRM Fund which was
inappropriately charged therein instead of charging it against the General
Fund.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
a) require the BAC to fast track the procurement process of LDRRM projects;
b) appoint a MDRRM Officer to facilitate the prompt implementation of
LDRRM projects of the Municipality to improve the capacity of the
Municipality to respond to emergencies or disasters.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to fully institutionalize the LDRRMO
by filling up the statutory/mandatory positions required under NDRRMC-DILG-
DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) 2014-1 to assure
implementation of actions and measures related to disaster risk reduction and
management.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor and the local sanggunian to (a) craft
ordinances and issue executive orders related to waste management and
environmental protection; (b) continuously improve the current waste
management system of the Municipality, including, garbage classification,
collection, and recycling; (c) add more waste recycling facilities; and (d) focus
on the recycling methods of the recyclables.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer
to intensify collection of past due accounts by sending demand letters citing
the municipal ordinance. She is likewise encouraged to design a collection
strategy that is both beneficial to the consumers and the BOMWASA, taking
into consideration the accessibility of the water collectors in the barangay
while at the same time complying rules and regulations on accountability of
public funds of collecting officers.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Human Resource
Management Officer to adhere to the guidelines of CSC Memorandum
Circular No. 17 s. 2002 and Section 77 of RA No. 7160.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to direct the GAD Focal Person to
conduct gender analysis in the formulation of programs and projects and
activities so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services,
protection and other assistance that may be provided.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant
to adapt a monitoring mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be
remitted and withheld. Furthermore, we also recommended that accounting
records such as cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash
disbursement journal, general and subsidiary ledgers must be updated monthly
in order to determine the exact amount to be remitted to the Bureau of Internal
Revenue.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require:

before the dates prescribed by GSIS;
b. the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant adapt a monitoring
mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld;
and
c. the Municipal Accountant regularly update accounting records such as
cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement
journal, general and subsidiary ledgers in order to determine the exact
amount to be remitted to the GSIS.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require:
1. the Municipal Treasurer to fully remit the Philhealth contributions withheld;
2. the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to adapt a monitoring
mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld;
and

cash receipts journal, check disbursement journal, cash disbursement
journal, general and subsidiary ledgers in order to determine the exact
amount to be remitted to the PHIC.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to direct:

mechanism to detect variances in the amount to be remitted and withheld;

journal, general and subsidiary ledgers in order to determine the exact

ambulance.

observations.recommendation:
1.17. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
 Direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to
reconcile their records, review the composition of the PPE account to come
up with an accurate Property, Plant and Equipment account balance; and
 Direct the Municipal Accountant to record in the book of accounts donated
PPEs found during physical count.

2.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
submit regularly the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements within the time
frame provided for in COA Circular No. 96-011, and effect corrections both in
the Municipal books of accounts and the bank, if any, to ascertain the accuracy
and correctness of the cash account.

3.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to pay only claims covered by Disbursement
Vouchers (DVs) together with the required supporting documents;
b) Direct the Municipal Accountant to record only transactions covered by
DVs and the required supporting documents; and
c) Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all claims recorded in the
book of accounts covered with valid DVs duly supported with the required
supporting documents are submitted to the concerned COA Auditors for
audit.

4.11. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
review the validity of the unclaimed obligations or accounts payable which are
outstanding for more than two years and if found to have no valid claims against
the municipality, request the Commission On Audit for the reversion of the
same to the Government Equity.

5.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Require the Municipal Accountant to send within ten (10) days before the
expiration of the prescribed period a written reminder under signature of the Mayor or his duly authorized representative enjoining officials and
employees concerned to settle their cash advance.
b) Provide this office a copy of the 2nd demand letter duly received by the
Accountable Officers for this office to take appropriate action;
c) Cause the immediate withholding of salaries and claims of officers and
employees who failed to settle their cash advances within the prescribed
period pursuant to Section 5.1.3 and 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002
after being sent notice and demand. The Municipal Accountant is
authorized to delete the name of the official and/or employee from the
subsequent payroll until such time that the cash advance has been fully
liquidated pursuant to COA Circular 96-004.
d) Direct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal
Budget Officer to refrain from signing cash advance vouchers and
obligation requests of officials and employees with outstanding cash
advances.

6.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and
Municipal Accountant to exert effort to enforce immediate settlement of the
audit disallowances and charges and ensure that all employees who are retiring
or transferring to other agencies shall first settle the disallowances and charges
for which they are liable in accordance with Section 7 of COA Circular 2009-
006 that the settlement of disallowances and charges is made within the
prescribed period.

7.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to
expedite the processing of documents for the titling of lands owned by the
Municipality. We further recommended that before any construction is made,
land ownership thru a certificate of title should be obtained.

8.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to maintain a special account in the General Fund for the 20% DF and
subsidiary ledgers for information, reporting and monitoring purposes.

9.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that only projects aligned
with the purpose/objectives of the 20% DF be funded from such appropriation
and adhere to DILG/DBM Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2017-1
dated February 22, 2017.

10.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Local Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Officer to coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant pertaining to available funds in the LDRRMF-Special Trust Fund
so that the full amount could be programmed in the LDRRMFIP.

11.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to transfer all unexpended balance of LDRRMF as of year-end to the required
Special Trust Fund account as required to protect the fund against misuse.

12.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to reclassify the balance of the DRRM Fund of P 2,223,187.08 recorded as Due
to LGUs account to Trust Liability-DRRM Fund as required by regulations.

13.6. We reiterated our recommendation that the CMRF be fenced with a secured
gate at the entrance to minimize illegal visitors or scavengers. This will also
help address the occupational health and safety issues by limiting access.

14.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) cause the allocation of funds
to purchase the appropriate safety clothing as prescribed in the Manual for
Operation and Maintenance at Sanitary Landfill; (b) require the SWM Focal
Person to ensure that any person entering the MRF must wear the proper safety
clothing so that health risks are minimized and MRF operations are safe.

15.6. We recommended that a mechanism to measure the volume of waste and type
of waste should be placed at the site, which will provide baseline data to assist
in developing effective waste minimization strategies and future plans for
waste management.

16.7. We reiterate our recommendation that the cover should be done on a daily
basis for biodegradable and compostable wastes. Waste in the MRF should not
be left open and strewn all over the place to protect health of the community
and the environment.

17.10. We recommended that a proper gate at the entrance to the MRF must be built
so that it is easier for the personnel at the MRF to stop vehicles coming to the
MRF at the gate, inspect the waste to confirm the type of waste to be disposed
then direct the vehicle to the designated area depending on the type of waste
already inspected. This daily process will compensate for the non-segregation
of waste at source and will ensure that wastes being disposed at the MRF are
properly separated.
17.11. We further recommended that the SEM Focal Person should implement a color
coding or appropriate means of identification to ensure that wastes are properly
segregated at the MRF.

18.6. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Solid Waste
Management Focal Person to maintain disposal site records.

19.6. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to maintain a separate book of accounts for BOMWASA and prepare periodic
reports thereof, for monitoring purposes.

20.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Members of the Board
of BOMWASA to implement Section 5 (b) of unnumbered Appropriation
Ordinance covered by SB Resolution No. 0137, s.-’04 dated December 13,
2004 on the replenishment of the 20% DF equivalent to the water system loan
amortization.

21.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
and Treasurer to transfer the gains from BOMWASA operation to the general
fund as set forth by Section 5( c ) of unnumbered Appropriation Ordinance
covered by SB Resolution No. 0137, s.-’04 dated December 13, 2004.

22.8. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource
Management Officer to adhere to the provisions set forth in Section 77 of RA
No. 7160.

23.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to
establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System

24.6. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the GAD Focal Person to
train personnel to man the VAW Help Desks in the barangays to capacitate
them to handle cases of violence against women in a gender-responsive
manner.

25.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
and Municipal Treasurer to carefully review remittance of withholding taxes
before the issuance of check for the remittance to ensure that the remittance is
equal to the amount withheld and remittance should be as prescribed by BIR
regulations, that is, on the 10th day of the month following the month in which
withholding was made.

26.6. We recommended that disbursement vouchers on remittance of BIR
withholding taxes should be supported with the BIR Official Receipt as proof
of remittance.

observations.managementAction: Some projects were fast-tracked and liquidation of funds initiated.
observations.reason: Some projects were implemented while others remained delayed due to procurement and implementation issues.

observations.recommendation: We enjoined the local officials for the utilization of the unexpended balance for disaster risk reduction and management program, project & activities and include the activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF of the previous years which were transferred to the Special Trust Fund in the LDRRMFIP.
observations.reason: Some unexpended balances were identified but not all were included in LDRRMFIP; planning for utilization ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality faithfully comply with the procurement procedures prescribed under R.A. 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations. We further recommended that the concerned officials exercise due diligence in the processing of claims, and ensure complete documentation to support legality of transactions before effecting payment.
observations.managementAction: The BAC and responsible officials were directed to comply with RA 9184 requirements and ensure proper warranty securities.
observations.reason: Some procurements corrected but prior transactions still had documentation and warranty shortfalls.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that a meticulous, careful and judicious planning of procurement activities be done by the BAC before the Approved Budget of the Contract will be determined so as to arrive at a more reasonable price of goods. Likewise, in the selection of suppliers/contractors, the BAC should be guided by a reasonable eligibility criteria to come up with eligible and qualified suppliers for an effective and efficient procurement system.
observations.managementAction: BAC instructed to adhere to eligibility criteria and exercise due diligence in evaluating bidders; future procurements corrected.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Treasurer and Accountant to observe strictly COA Circular No. 2012-001 and that all fund transfers must have a Memorandum of Agreement to include a provision that the infrastructure project must be validated upon completion of the project through the preparation of an inspection report by the Municipal Engineer based on the statement of work accomplished prepared by the barangay to be attached to the liquidation report.
observations.managementAction: Some monitoring mechanisms instituted; Municipal Engineer to validate completed works.
observations.reason: Monitoring improved but not all recipient schools submitted required completion/inspection reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the strict observance to the provisions of Section 272 and 100 of the LGC, Article 363 and Item 4 of Joint Circular No. 01, s. 1998 of the DECS, DBM and DILG, in the utilization of the Special Education Fund.
observations.managementAction: Municipal officials were instructed to observe the provisions and ensure SEF used only for authorized purposes.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive: (a) provide the necessary support in enjoining the barangays to establish their own MRFs and to implement waste segregation at source; (b) strictly enforce and intensify the information campaign on the no-littering policy among the residents; (c) consider embarking on effective projects and programs to support the LGU's ecological solid waste management program; (d) strictly impose penalty to those households who refuse to segregate the non and biodegradable waste materials; and (e) strictly comply with all other prescribed procedures and guidelines under RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: Some IEC campaigns and support to barangays for MRF establishment were undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the municipality may initially pilot a single barangay to establish MRF project in order to institute a system of knowledge in one as well as to gain commitment/participation of barangay officials on the proper administration and operation of standard based MRF.
observations.managementAction: Pilot MRF projects and IEC activities were initiated in selected barangays.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the strict observance to the provisions of Section 272 and 100 of the LGC, Article 363 of its Implementing Rules and Regulations and Item 4 of Joint Circular No. 01, s. 1998 of the DECS, DBM and DILG, in the utilization of the Special Education Fund.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented: some expenditures continued but management agreed to allocate public school expenses from the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended measures to address these deficiencies: the maximum borrowing capacity should consider not only the 20% debt service ceiling but also the LGU’s net available funds prior to contracting of loans/borrowings; subsidiary ledgers should be maintained for each loan; LGU should conduct self-assessment of capacity to repay the loan considering net available funds and ensure utilization of loan proceeds is covered by appropriation ordinance; determine most cost effective borrowing scheme and allocate sufficient funds to cover total contracted liabilities.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented: recommendations discussed; Municipal Treasurer agreed to adopt measures in the future.
observations.reason: Continuous need to adopt recommended measures; full implementation pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require officials concerned to submit a copy of the duly perfected government contracts/purchase orders, irrespective of amount, and all supporting documents thereto, to the audit team within five (5) working days from issuance thereof. The Purchase Order must be legible in all respects and should contain all necessary data and information.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented: some POs and supporting documents still not timely submitted though procedures were reminded to offices.
observations.reason: Continued lapses in timely submission of perfected contracts and supporting documents; compliance remains pending.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the officials concerned
to conduct physical inventories of fixed assets at least once a year in accordance with
the guidelines enumerated under COA circular No.80-124 dated January 18, 1980.
Further, we recommended that the LCE create a Committee who will be in-
charge in processing and securing Certificates of Title for real properties acquired by
the LGU to ensure complete documentation to support the ownership claim of the
LGU on said properties.

We recommended that implementation of projects and acquisition of PPEs be
properly recorded to reflect in the financial statements the acquired properties, as well
as other uses to implement them, in order for the account PPE, and affected expense
accounts be fairly reflected in the agency’s books and financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the prompt
implementation of BUB Programs/Projects/Activities within the timeframe set by the
Source Agencies to address the poverty reduction objective of the program.
Moreover, as soon as projects are completed, it is recommended that prompt
liquidation of these funds be rendered to the source agencies to facilitate release of
other funds programmed for subsequent implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor submit the following as basis
in the post-audit transactions in the implementation of DA funded BUB projects,
and all other similar funds, viz:
a) Memorandum of Agreements/Understanding (MOAs/MOUs) on the
funds released;
b) Documents forming part thereof by reference or incorporation such as
Work and Financial Plan, etc. shall be furnished to the Auditor of the
agency concerned within five (5) working days from the execution of
the contract.

We enjoined the local officials for the utilization of the unexpended balance for
disaster risk reduction and management program, project & activities and include the
activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF of the previous years which were
transferred to the Special Trust Fund in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the Municipality faithfully comply with the
procurement procedures prescribed under R.A. 9184 and it’s implementing rules
and regulations. We further recommend that the concerned officials exercise due
diligence in the processing of claims, and ensure complete documentation to
support legality of transactions and their validity/correctness before effecting
payment.

b) Surety Bond issued in lieu of the withdrawn 10% Retention money is not
sufficient to cover the 1 year defects liability period upon completion of the
project.
Section 9.1.1.5 of Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184,
provides:
9.1.1.5 Release of Retention Money
General Guidelines
The total retention money shall be due for release upon final
acceptance of the works. The contractor may, however, request the
substitution of the retention money for each progress billing and
irrevocable standby letters of credit from a commercial bank, bank
guarantees or surety bond callable on demand, of amounts equivalent to
the retention money substituted for and accepted to the Government,
provided that the project is on schedule and is satisfactorily undertaken
(Annex E of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184)
Additional Documentary Requirements
o Any security in the form of cash, bank guarantee,
irrevocable standby letter of credit from a commercial
bank, GSIS or surety bond callable on demand
o Certification from the end-user that the project is completed
and inspected
Likewise, Section 62 of Revised Implementing Rules and Regulation of
R.A. 9184 states:
62.2. For the procurement of infrastructure projects, the following
warranties shall be made:
62.2.1.From the time project construction commenced up to final
acceptance, the contractor shall assume full responsibility for the
following:
a) any damage or destruction of the works except those
occasioned by force majeure; and
b) safety, protection, security, and convenience of his
personnel, third parties, and the public at large, as well
as the works, equipment, installation and the like to be
affected by his construction work;
62.2.2. One (1) year from project completion up to final acceptance or the
defects liability period.
62.2.3.3. To guarantee that the contractor shall perform his
responsibilities as prescribed in Section 62.2.3.1 (a) of this IRR, it
shall be required to post a warranty security in accordance with
the following schedule:

For biddings conducted by LGUs, the contractor may also
submit warranty securities in the form of bank guarantee or
letter of credit from other banks certified by the BSP as
authorized to issue such financial instrument.
62.2.3.4. The warranty security shall be denominated in Philippine
Pesos, remain effective for one (1) year from the date of
issuance of the Certificate of Final Acceptance by the
procuring entity, and returned only after the lapse of the said
one (1) year period.
Therefore, a contractor should post the following warranties:
a) Retention Money is collected during progress payment and serves as
a warranty against hidden or patent defects. It is likewise retuned by
the procuring entity upon issuance of Certificate of Acceptance, unless
sooner replaced by an acceptable form of security (surety bond or
irrevocable letter of credit).
b) Warranty Security is posted when the Certificate of Acceptance is
issued and shall be valid for one year or the defects liability period;
thereafter it shall be returned by the procuring entity. It serves as a
warranty against structural defects or structural failures.
It was also revealed during our review of documents that the guarantee
issued by the contractor in the form of surety bond upon withdrawal of 10%
retention was only 10% of the contract price of the project which was below the
required amount specified in the aforementioned provision of IRR of RA 9184, as
shown below:
